text: This is a video of a laparoscopic intraperitoneal mesh repair for a recurrent incisional hernia using the BARD echo positioning system.
A seventy-six year old male with a previous laparotomy for small bowel obstruction followed by an open onlay mesh repair of an incisional hernia several years before.
He presented to our clinic with a symptomatic recurrent incisional hernia.
The laparoscopic view reveals multiple defects in the abdominal wall, indicative of a recurrent incisional hernia.
The surgeon places two 10mm ports in the right and left upper quadrants and two 5mm ports at the level of the umbilicus for the laparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection to reduce the hernia contents, carefully separating and repositioning the tissue within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon inserts the mesh through a ten millimeter port using a prepackaged tool that tightly rolls the mesh for easy insertion into the abdominal cavity.
A suture passer is introduced into the center of the hernia defect, and the balloon caster located in the center of the mesh is grasped and pulled to the exterior.
The surgeon uses a pre-packaged syringe system to inflate the balloon with air, causing the mesh to spread. The mesh is then lifted and positioned as required within the surgical field.
This has the advantage of saving the time and effort without the need for placement of orientation sutures on the conventional laparoscopic mesh repair.
The surgeon demonstrates the ease of manipulating the mesh within the surgical field, adjusting its position over the hernia defect.
The surgeon uses absorbo tucks to place circumferential tucks around the balloon, securing the mesh in position within the hernia defect.
The surgeon completes the placement of a full circumferential layer of tacks around the mesh, securing it in position within the hernia defect.
The surgeon deflates the balloon and extracts it smoothly through the ten millimeter port.
Pressure is applied externally to counteract the tacks during the mesh placement step in the hernia repair procedure.
The inner layer of the mesh is visible, composed of a hydrophilic material designed to be bowel-friendly.
Following placement of this row.
After the balloon is removed, the surgeon applies a second layer of circumferential tacks around the hernia repair site.
Non-absorbable tacks are applied as a second layer to secure the mesh in place during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon secures the mesh to the abdominal wall using non-absorbable tacks, ensuring stable fixation within the surgical field.
The mesh is shown in its final position, securely covering all visible defects in the abdominal wall.
These are a diagram of the components used followed by illustrations on operation of this mesh.
We have found that this has been very useful in our practice.
